clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman with a 3-year history of SLE (on hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisolone) presents for routine follow-up. She has no new symptoms but her urine dipstick shows 3+ protein and 2+ blood. Serum creatinine is 118 µmol/L (previously normal). Anti-dsDNA titre has risen from 1:40 to 1:320. Serum C3 is low at 0.6 g/L. Renal biopsy reveals diffuse proliferative lupus nephritis (ISN/RPS Class IV). She asks about her long-term kidney outlook. Which factor in her presentation carries the WORST prognostic implication for renal survival at 10 years?
options: Elevated anti-dsDNA titre
Low serum C3 complement
ISN/RPS Class IV (diffuse proliferative) nephritis on biopsy
Proteinuria of 3+ on dipstick
Mild rise in serum creatinine to 118 µmol/L
explanation: ISN/RPS Class IV diffuse proliferative lupus nephritis is the most severe histological class and carries the worst long-term renal prognosis, with up to 20–30% of patients progressing to end-stage renal disease within 10 years despite treatment. While elevated anti-dsDNA, low complement, proteinuria, and rising creatinine all reflect active disease, the biopsy class is the single strongest predictor of renal outcome and guides intensity of immunosuppressive therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Elevated anti-dsDNA is a useful disease activity marker and can predict flares, but it is a serological marker rather than a direct determinant of long-term renal survival; biopsy class supersedes serology for prognosis.

Low C3 reflects complement consumption in active lupus nephritis and correlates with disease activity, but it is not the strongest independent predictor of long-term renal survival compared with histological class.

Class IV (diffuse proliferative) nephritis is correct — it affects >50% of glomeruli, carries the highest risk of progression to end-stage renal disease, and mandates aggressive induction immunosuppression.

Proteinuria of 3+ is an important clinical feature and itself worsens prognosis (nephrotic-range proteinuria >3.5 g/day is a poor marker), but it is the consequence of the underlying nephritis class rather than the primary prognostic determinant.

A creatinine of 118 µmol/L represents only a mild rise and is not in itself the worst prognostic feature; impaired baseline renal function at diagnosis is more prognostically ominous, but the histological class drives long-term outlook.
clinicalPearl: Biopsy class is the single most important prognostic factor in lupus nephritis — Class IV (diffuse proliferative) carries the highest risk of end-stage renal disease and mandates aggressive induction therapy with cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate.
keyClue: Renal biopsy showing ISN/RPS Class IV (diffuse proliferative) lupus nephritis in a young woman with active SLE serology.
commonExamTrap: Focusing on the serological markers (anti-dsDNA rise, low C3) as prognostic determinants — the renal biopsy class is the definitive prognostic and therapeutic guide.
missedConcept: Histological classification of lupus nephritis (ISN/RPS Classes I–VI) and the prognostic hierarchy: Class IV > Class III > Class V regarding risk of progression to ESRD.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman with known SLE presents after her third consecutive first-trimester miscarriage. She has no history of thrombosis. Her current medications are hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisolone. Blood tests show: ANA positive, anti-dsDNA 1:80, lupus anticoagulant POSITIVE on two occasions 12 weeks apart, anti-cardiolipin IgG 65 GPL units (high positive), anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I IgG strongly positive. She asks what caused her miscarriages and what treatment will give her the best chance of a successful pregnancy. What is the MOST appropriate management going forward?
options: Increase prednisolone to 40 mg/day before the next pregnancy attempt
Add azathioprine as a steroid-sparing agent and plan IVF
Low-dose aspirin started before conception combined with low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) once pregnancy confirmed
Warfarin anticoagulation throughout the entire pregnancy
No change in therapy — miscarriages in SLE are due to disease activity; control the SLE and the pregnancies will succeed
explanation: This woman meets criteria for antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) — three or more consecutive miscarriages plus persistently positive antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin IgG, anti-beta-2-GP1 IgG all positive on two occasions). The standard of care for obstetric APS to improve live birth rate is low-dose aspirin (75 mg) started before conception combined with prophylactic LMWH once pregnancy is confirmed, continued throughout pregnancy. This combination reduces placental thrombosis and increases live birth rates from ~10% to approximately 70–80%.
wrongAnswerExplanations: High-dose steroids are not indicated for obstetric APS; they do not address the thrombotic/prothrombotic mechanism of antiphospholipid antibodies and cause significant maternal morbidity without proven benefit for miscarriage prevention.
Azathioprine and IVF do not address the antiphospholipid-mediated thrombosis in the uteroplacental vasculature; IVF without anticoagulant cover carries very high risk of further losses in APS.

Low-dose aspirin plus LMWH is correct — this is the guideline-recommended regimen for obstetric APS, with evidence of significantly improved live birth outcomes compared with either agent alone or no treatment.

Warfarin is absolutely contraindicated in the first trimester (weeks 6–12) due to embryopathy (nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses, CNS anomalies) and in late pregnancy due to fetal haemorrhage; LMWH is used instead.

Miscarriages in this setting are due to antiphospholipid antibody-mediated placental thrombosis, not simply SLE disease activity; they will not be prevented by better SLE control alone without targeted anticoagulant therapy.
clinicalPearl: Obstetric APS (recurrent miscarriage + positive antiphospholipid antibodies) requires low-dose aspirin pre-conception plus LMWH once pregnant — this combination raises the live birth rate from ~10% to ~75%; warfarin is teratogenic and contraindicated in the first trimester.
keyClue: Three consecutive miscarriages + persistently positive lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin, and anti-beta-2-GP1 IgG on two occasions = antiphospholipid syndrome requiring aspirin + LMWH.
commonExamTrap: Using warfarin in pregnancy for APS — warfarin crosses the placenta and is teratogenic; LMWH is the safe anticoagulant in pregnancy.
missedConcept: Antiphospholipid syndrome as a complication of SLE and the specific obstetric management with aspirin plus LMWH; warfarin's contraindication in the first trimester.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman has been taking hydroxychloroquine 400 mg/day for 7 years for SLE. She is in clinical remission with no active disease. She attends for routine follow-up and asks whether any monitoring is needed for her long-term medication. On review, she has no visual complaints. Her weight is 62 kg (making her actual dose 6.45 mg/kg/day). What is the MOST important monitoring investigation she requires and at what interval?
options: Annual liver function tests and full blood count — hepatotoxicity and cytopenias are the main concerns
Annual renal function tests — hydroxychloroquine is renally cleared and accumulates in renal impairment
Annual ophthalmological assessment including visual field testing and spectral-domain OCT — screening for retinal toxicity
Biannual ECG monitoring — hydroxychloroquine causes QT prolongation as a class effect
No monitoring required — hydroxychloroquine has an excellent safety profile and does not require routine surveillance at standard doses
explanation: Hydroxychloroquine can cause irreversible retinal toxicity (bull's-eye maculopathy) with long-term use, particularly at doses above 5 mg/kg/day and after 5 or more years of use. This patient exceeds the safe dosing threshold at 6.45 mg/kg/day. Annual ophthalmological screening with visual field testing (Humphrey 10-2) and spectral-domain OCT is recommended after 5 years of use (or sooner at high-dose/high-risk patients) to detect pre-symptomatic retinal changes before vision is lost, as the damage is irreversible.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Hepatotoxicity and significant cytopenias are not characteristic adverse effects of hydroxychloroquine; LFTs and FBC are not the primary monitoring concern for this drug.

Renal monitoring is relevant as a disease parameter in SLE but is not the primary pharmacological monitoring requirement for hydroxychloroquine itself; the drug's main organ toxicity is retinal, not renal.

Annual ophthalmological review with visual fields and SD-OCT is correct — this is guideline-recommended (AAO/BSR) after 5 years of therapy or earlier in high-risk patients (dose >5 mg/kg/day, renal impairment, pre-existing maculopathy).

QT prolongation is a recognised risk with hydroxychloroquine, especially at supratherapeutic doses or combined with other QT-prolonging drugs, but routine annual ECG is not a standard monitoring recommendation in stable patients on therapeutic doses.
Hydroxychloroquine does require monitoring — retinal toxicity is a serious, dose-dependent, and irreversible complication; the AAO recommends annual screening after 5 years of use.
clinicalPearl: Hydroxychloroquine causes irreversible retinal toxicity (bull's-eye maculopathy) — annual ophthalmology screening with Humphrey 10-2 visual fields and SD-OCT is mandatory after 5 years of therapy or earlier if dose exceeds 5 mg/kg/day.
keyClue: Seven years of hydroxychloroquine at 6.45 mg/kg/day (above the 5 mg/kg/day safe threshold) — retinal toxicity screening is overdue.
commonExamTrap: Focusing on haematological or renal monitoring — the signature long-term toxicity of hydroxychloroquine is retinal, not hepatic or renal.
missedConcept: Hydroxychloroquine retinal toxicity risk increases with duration (>5 years) and dose (>5 mg/kg/day); pre-symptomatic screening with SD-OCT can detect changes before irreversible vision loss.

clinicalStem: A 42-year-old woman with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (dcSSc) diagnosed 18 months ago presents to her GP with a 2-day history of severe headache, blurred vision, and oliguria. She has been on high-dose prednisolone (40 mg/day) for 3 months for inflammatory myopathy. Her blood pressure is 178/112 mmHg (previously 120/76 mmHg). Serum creatinine is 380 µmol/L (baseline 85 µmol/L). Urine shows haematuria and proteinuria. Peripheral blood film shows fragmented red cells (schistocytes). What is the MOST important immediate step in her management?
options: Increase prednisolone dose to 60 mg/day to control the systemic sclerosis flare
Urgent hospital admission and start of an ACE inhibitor (captopril) without waiting for nephrology review
Emergency referral to hospital for investigation of hypertensive emergency; start ACE inhibitor (captopril) immediately and continue it even if dialysis is required
Start amlodipine for blood pressure control and review in 48 hours
Urgent plasma exchange for microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia as the primary intervention
explanation: This presentation is classic scleroderma renal crisis (SRC) — acute hypertensive emergency with rapid-onset renal failure, microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA), and oliguria in a patient with diffuse SSc on high-dose steroids (a major risk factor for SRC). ACE inhibitors (captopril) are the cornerstone of treatment and have transformed survival; they must be started immediately, titrated aggressively, and continued even if the patient requires dialysis — approximately 50% of patients who require dialysis can recover renal function if ACE inhibitors are maintained. Steroids should be tapered, not increased. Emergency hospital admission is imperative given the severity.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Increasing steroids is the opposite of correct management — high-dose corticosteroids are a recognised precipitant of scleroderma renal crisis; the dose should be tapered urgently.
ACE inhibitor therapy is correct but this option omits the need for emergency hospital admission and specialist involvement; given creatinine of 380 µmol/L, hospital-level care is essential.

This is the correct answer — emergency admission, immediate ACE inhibitor (captopril titrated), and continuing ACE inhibitor even through dialysis is the evidence-based standard of care for scleroderma renal crisis.

Amlodipine alone is inadequate — calcium channel blockers do not address the reno-protective mechanism of ACE inhibition in SRC and a 48-hour review is dangerous given end-organ damage already present.

Plasma exchange is used in TTP (thrombocytopenia + MAHA); SRC is treated primarily with ACE inhibitors, not plasma exchange — the distinction is critical and the two conditions require different treatments.
clinicalPearl: Scleroderma renal crisis — new hypertension + acute renal failure in diffuse SSc (especially on steroids) — requires immediate ACE inhibitor therapy (captopril); continue ACE inhibitor even if dialysis is needed, as ~50% recover renal function.
keyClue: Diffuse SSc + high-dose steroid use + acute severe hypertension + rapidly rising creatinine + MAHA (schistocytes) = scleroderma renal crisis.
commonExamTrap: Increasing steroids or choosing plasma exchange — steroids worsen SRC; plasma exchange is for TTP. ACE inhibitors are the life-saving treatment for SRC.
missedConcept: Scleroderma renal crisis is precipitated by high-dose steroids; ACE inhibitors are disease-modifying and should be continued through dialysis as renal recovery is possible.
redFlagReason: Scleroderma renal crisis is a hypertensive emergency with rapidly progressive renal failure and MAHA; delayed or incorrect treatment (steroids, withholding ACE inhibitor) is life-threatening. Immediate hospital admission and ACE inhibitor therapy are mandatory.

topic: Pulmonary arterial hypertension in systemic sclerosis
clinicalStem: A 54-year-old woman with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (lcSSc, anti-centromere antibody positive) of 12 years duration presents for annual review. She has had longstanding Raynaud's phenomenon, skin thickening limited to the hands and face, and intermittent dysphagia. Recently she has noticed progressive exertional dyspnoea over the past 4 months; she can no longer climb one flight of stairs without stopping. SpO2 is 96% at rest. Auscultation reveals no crackles and her chest X-ray is clear. ECG shows right axis deviation. What is the MOST appropriate next investigation to screen for the most likely serious complication?
options: High-resolution CT chest to look for interstitial lung disease
Echocardiography to estimate pulmonary artery pressure and screen for pulmonary arterial hypertension
Exercise tolerance testing to quantify functional limitation
Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan to exclude chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension
Anti-topoisomerase I (anti-Scl-70) antibody testing to reassess antibody profile
explanation: Limited cutaneous SSc (especially with anti-centromere antibodies) after 10+ years carries a 10–15% lifetime risk of pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), which is a leading cause of death in this subtype. The clinical triad of progressive exertional dyspnoea + clear chest (no ILD crackles) + right axis deviation on ECG in this context strongly points to PAH rather than ILD. Echocardiography is the recommended first-line screening investigation for PAH in SSc, estimating RVSP/tricuspid regurgitation gradient. Annual echo screening is guideline-recommended in all SSc patients.
wrongAnswerExplanations: HRCT is indicated to look for interstitial lung disease, which is more common in diffuse SSc with anti-Scl-70 antibodies; in lcSSc with anti-centromere antibodies and no basal crackles, PAH is the more likely complication and echo takes priority.

Echocardiography is correct — it is the recommended screening investigation for PAH in SSc, and the clinical picture (progressive dyspnoea, clear chest, right axis deviation, anti-centromere lcSSc) points squarely to PAH.
Exercise testing quantifies functional status but does not diagnose or characterise the underlying cause of dyspnoea; it should not precede echocardiography in this scenario.

V/Q scan screens for CTEPH (chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension), which is a possibility but less likely than PAH in this clinical context; echo should precede V/Q scanning in the SSc screening pathway.
Anti-Scl-70 is associated with ILD in diffuse SSc; this patient is already known to be anti-centromere positive, and repeating antibody testing does not address the acute diagnostic concern.
clinicalPearl: In limited cutaneous SSc (especially anti-centromere antibody positive), PAH is a leading late complication — annual echocardiography screening is guideline-recommended, and unexplained progressive dyspnoea with clear lungs should trigger immediate echo.
keyClue: lcSSc + anti-centromere positive + 12 years disease + progressive exertional dyspnoea + clear chest X-ray + right axis deviation = PAH until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Ordering HRCT first — ILD is the more common complication in diffuse SSc with anti-Scl-70; PAH dominates in lcSSc with anti-centromere antibodies and a clear chest X-ray.
missedConcept: Antibody-organ complication associations in SSc: anti-centromere → PAH and CREST; anti-Scl-70 → ILD and dcSSc; echo is the first-line PAH screening tool.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis presents in winter with a painful, blackened tip of her right index finger that has been present for 2 weeks. She is on nifedipine 30 mg modified-release daily for Raynaud's phenomenon, which has given partial benefit. The affected fingertip is cold and non-blanching; the digit distal to the proximal interphalangeal joint is dusky. She is systemically well and afebrile. There is no fluctuance or pus. What is the MOST appropriate next step in management?
options: Increase nifedipine to 60 mg/day and review in 2 weeks
Urgent hospital referral for intravenous iloprost infusion
Immediate digital amputation to prevent proximal spread of gangrene
Add sildenafil 25 mg three times daily as an outpatient and review in 1 week
Apply topical glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) paste to the digit and arrange physiotherapy
explanation: This patient has digital ischaemia with threatened digit loss in SSc — a critical vascular emergency. Intravenous iloprost (a prostacyclin analogue) is the evidence-based treatment for severe digital ischaemia/ulcers not responding to oral vasodilators in SSc. It causes potent vasodilation, reduces platelet aggregation, and is given as an inpatient infusion over several days. EULAR guidelines recommend IV iloprost for SSc digital ulcers and critical ischaemia when oral therapy fails. Urgent hospital referral is essential.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Simply increasing nifedipine is inadequate for critical digital ischaemia with threatened tissue loss — outpatient management is inappropriate when a digit is at imminent risk; IV therapy is needed.
Urgent hospital referral for IV iloprost is correct — it provides rapid, powerful vasodilation and has evidence for healing SSc digital ulcers and preventing critical ischaemia progression.

Amputation should be avoided if vasodilator therapy can salvage the digit; it is a last resort after medical therapy has failed and demarcation is complete — premature amputation in SSc is inappropriate.

Oral sildenafil is useful for prevention of SSc digital ulcers and Raynaud's management but is not indicated as sole therapy for established critical ischaemia with threatened digit loss; IV iloprost is superior in this acute setting.
Topical GTN may help mild Raynaud's but is not appropriate management for critical ischaemia with blackened digit — this would delay definitive treatment.
clinicalPearl: Critical digital ischaemia with threatened digit loss in SSc — failing oral vasodilators — requires urgent hospital referral for intravenous iloprost infusion; early treatment prevents amputation.
keyClue: Blackened, non-blanching, cold fingertip for 2 weeks in diffuse SSc on nifedipine = critical ischaemia requiring IV iloprost.
commonExamTrap: Escalating oral therapy or opting for topical treatment when the digit is critically ischaemic — IV iloprost is the standard of care for critical ischaemia in SSc.
missedConcept: The escalation pathway for Raynaud's/digital ischaemia in SSc: conservative → oral CCB/PDE5i → IV iloprost → surgical sympathectomy; IV iloprost is the rescue therapy for critical ischaemia.

topic: Axial spondyloarthropathy — cardiovascular risk and follow-up
clinicalStem: A 44-year-old man with ankylosing spondylitis (HLA-B27 positive) has been on naproxen 500 mg twice daily for 6 years with reasonable symptom control. He attends for annual review and mentions occasional dyspnoea on exertion but attributes it to deconditioning. He is a non-smoker. BP is 138/86 mmHg. Pulse is regular at 92 bpm with a faint early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge. ECG shows PR interval of 240 ms. CRP is 18 mg/L. What complication of ankylosing spondylitis does this presentation suggest and what is the MOST appropriate next investigation?
options: Costochondritis causing dyspnoea — reassure and continue current treatment
NSAID-induced cardiomyopathy — stop naproxen and request troponin
Aortic regurgitation and first-degree heart block due to aortic root inflammation — urgent echocardiography
Pulmonary fibrosis due to ankylosing spondylitis — request high-resolution CT chest
Hypertensive heart disease — start antihypertensive therapy and repeat ECG in 3 months
explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis causes aortitis involving the aortic root, leading to aortic regurgitation (early diastolic murmur at left sternal edge) and conduction defects (first-degree or higher heart block, due to fibrosis involving the conduction system). These are well-recognised extra-articular cardiac complications of AS, occurring in up to 10% of patients with longstanding disease. The combination of early diastolic murmur + prolonged PR interval + exertional dyspnoea in a patient with AS requires urgent echocardiography to assess aortic root and valve function.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Costochondritis causes chest wall pain but not an early diastolic murmur or conduction defects; reassurance is inappropriate when cardiac pathology is evident on examination.

NSAIDs can cause fluid retention and cardiovascular events but do not cause the specific pattern of early diastolic murmur + first-degree heart block; NSAID-induced cardiomyopathy is not a recognised entity.

Aortic regurgitation with first-degree heart block in AS is correct — aortic root inflammation is the pathological mechanism, and echocardiography is the appropriate urgent investigation to quantify AR severity and aortic root dimensions.
Apical upper-lobe pulmonary fibrosis is a rare pulmonary complication of AS but would not explain the early diastolic murmur or prolonged PR interval; HRCT is not the priority investigation here.
While mild hypertension is present, it does not cause an early diastolic murmur; the combination of findings points to AS-related aortitis, not hypertensive heart disease.
clinicalPearl: Ankylosing spondylitis causes aortic root inflammation leading to aortic regurgitation (early diastolic murmur) and cardiac conduction defects (PR prolongation, heart block) — perform echocardiography at annual review and when new cardiac symptoms arise.
keyClue: Long-standing AS + early diastolic murmur at left sternal edge + first-degree heart block on ECG = AS-related aortitis with aortic regurgitation.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the murmur to hypertension or NSAIDs — the specific pattern of early diastolic murmur + conduction defect in AS is pathognomonic for aortic root involvement.
missedConcept: Extra-articular cardiac manifestations of ankylosing spondylitis: aortitis → aortic regurgitation; inflammation of conduction tissue → first-degree, second-degree, or complete heart block.

clinicalStem: A 36-year-old man with severe psoriatic arthritis has been established on adalimumab (anti-TNF biologic) for 14 months with excellent joint and skin response. He now presents with a 3-week history of productive cough, night sweats, 4 kg weight loss, and fatigue. He was born in a country with high TB prevalence and had a positive Mantoux test (18 mm) before starting adalimumab; he completed a full 9-month course of isoniazid prophylaxis prior to commencing the biologic. CXR shows a right upper lobe opacity with cavitation. What is the MOST appropriate immediate action?
options: Continue adalimumab — the isoniazid course provides lifelong protection against TB reactivation
Switch from adalimumab to ustekinumab (IL-12/23 inhibitor) which has a lower TB risk, and refer to respiratory medicine
Suspend adalimumab immediately, refer urgently to infectious disease/respiratory medicine, and initiate anti-tuberculosis therapy following sputum culture
Add clarithromycin for atypical pneumonia cover as the most likely diagnosis, and continue adalimumab
Repeat CXR in 4 weeks to assess for progression before making a management decision
explanation: This presentation is TB reactivation/primary TB in an immunosuppressed patient on anti-TNF therapy — a recognised and potentially fatal complication. TNF-alpha is critical for granuloma integrity, and anti-TNF agents dramatically increase the risk of TB reactivation. Despite completing isoniazid prophylaxis (which reduces but does not eliminate risk), the clinical and radiological picture (right upper lobe cavitation + constitutional symptoms + high-risk background) is highly suspicious for active TB. Adalimumab must be suspended immediately, urgent specialist review arranged, and sputum sent for AFB smear and culture. Anti-TB therapy should be initiated without delay.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Isoniazid prophylaxis significantly reduces but does not eliminate the lifetime risk of TB reactivation — continuing adalimumab in the context of likely active TB is dangerous and potentially fatal.

Switching to ustekinumab still represents immunosuppression and does not address the immediate clinical emergency; the biologic must be stopped and anti-TB treatment started regardless of the subsequent biologic choice.
Suspending adalimumab and starting anti-TB therapy is correct — anti-TNF agents must be withheld during active TB treatment; TNF blockade prevents granuloma formation and promotes dissemination.

The radiological appearance (upper lobe cavitation + constitutional symptoms) in this clinical context is TB until proven otherwise — atypical pneumonia is not the priority diagnosis and continuing adalimumab is contraindicated.
A 4-week delay to repeat CXR while a patient with possible active TB continues adalimumab is clinically unacceptable and potentially fatal; immediate action is required.
clinicalPearl: Anti-TNF therapy reactivates latent TB — any patient on a biologic with constitutional symptoms and upper lobe infiltrate/cavitation has TB until proven otherwise; suspend the biologic immediately and start anti-TB therapy after sputum collection.
keyClue: Right upper lobe cavitation + night sweats + weight loss + cough in a patient on adalimumab with high TB risk background = active tuberculosis requiring immediate biologic suspension.
commonExamTrap: Assuming completed isoniazid prophylaxis confers lifelong protection — it reduces risk by ~90% but not to zero; TB can still develop, especially on prolonged anti-TNF therapy.
missedConcept: Anti-TNF biologics increase TB risk 4–25-fold; pre-treatment LTBI screening and prophylaxis is mandatory, and any suspected active TB requires immediate biologic suspension regardless of prophylaxis history.
redFlagReason: Active tuberculosis in an immunosuppressed patient on anti-TNF therapy is a serious, potentially fatal complication requiring immediate biologic suspension, urgent specialist referral, and prompt anti-TB therapy — delay risks disseminated or miliary TB.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old man with a 30-year history of ankylosing spondylitis (syndesmophytes and bamboo spine on imaging) is brought to the emergency department after a low-energy fall in the garden, landing on his back. He complains of severe neck pain and paresthesiae in both arms. There is no obvious external deformity. Standard AP and lateral cervical X-rays appear normal. What is the MOST important next step?
options: Reassure the patient — normal X-rays exclude significant injury; prescribe analgesia and discharge
Immobilise the spine fully, admit, and arrange urgent CT spine (or MRI) — a fracture through the fused spinal column must be excluded despite normal plain films
Arrange outpatient physiotherapy referral for cervical muscle spasm
Repeat X-rays in 2 weeks to reassess for callus formation indicating an occult fracture
Start high-dose NSAIDs and refer to rheumatology outpatient clinic in 4 weeks
explanation: The fused bamboo spine in AS behaves like a long bone — low-energy trauma can cause transverse fractures through the rigid fused column (chance fracture or hyperextension fracture), often at the cervicothoracic or thoracolumbar junction, and plain X-rays frequently miss these injuries due to osteoporosis and overlying structures. Neurological symptoms (paresthesiae) raise immediate concern for cord involvement. This is a spinal cord emergency — full spinal immobilisation, urgent CT (or MRI if neurological deficit present), and admission are mandatory regardless of plain film appearance.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Normal plain X-rays do NOT exclude spinal fracture in AS — the sensitivity of plain films for fractures through a fused, osteoporotic spine is low; discharging this patient risks cord injury from an undetected unstable fracture.

Full spinal immobilisation + urgent CT/MRI is correct — AS spinal fractures are inherently unstable, frequently missed on plain films, and carry a high risk of spinal cord injury if the diagnosis is delayed.

Physiotherapy referral is completely inappropriate — the combination of trauma + neurological symptoms + AS bamboo spine requires urgent spinal imaging to exclude cord-threatening fracture before any mobilisation.
Repeating X-rays in 2 weeks delays diagnosis of a potentially unstable fracture with cord involvement — management cannot wait for callus formation.
NSAIDs and outpatient review are appropriate for an AS flare but are entirely inadequate for a patient with neurological symptoms after trauma in AS; this approach risks catastrophic cord injury.
clinicalPearl: In ankylosing spondylitis, even low-energy trauma can cause unstable spinal fractures through the rigid bamboo spine that are missed on plain X-rays — bilateral neurological symptoms after any trauma mandate full spinal immobilisation and urgent CT/MRI.
keyClue: Bamboo spine + low-energy fall + new bilateral paresthesiae + normal plain X-rays = unstable spinal fracture until proven otherwise by CT/MRI.
commonExamTrap: Being reassured by normal plain X-rays — in AS, the fused osteoporotic spine is prone to fractures invisible on plain film; CT is the gold standard.
missedConcept: AS patients with bamboo spine are at high risk of unstable fractures from minimal trauma; neurological symptoms after trauma constitute a spinal cord emergency requiring immobilisation and urgent cross-sectional imaging.
redFlagReason: Possible unstable cervical spine fracture with cord compression in AS — bilateral paresthesiae after trauma in a patient with bamboo spine is a spinal emergency; mismanagement risks complete cord injury.

topic: Giant cell arteritis — prevention of visual loss
clinicalStem: A 72-year-old woman was diagnosed with giant cell arteritis 8 months ago after presenting with headache, jaw claudication, and scalp tenderness (ESR 98 mm/hr, CRP 62 mg/L; temporal artery biopsy confirmed). She was started on prednisolone 60 mg/day with rapid symptom resolution. She is now on prednisolone 15 mg/day following a structured taper. Today she returns with a 5-day history of right temporal headache and jaw pain on chewing that has returned. Current ESR is 42 mm/hr and CRP is 28 mg/L. She has no visual symptoms. What is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Continue current prednisolone dose and repeat bloods in 4 weeks — minor elevation may be non-specific
Stop prednisolone and refer to rheumatology for alternative immunosuppression
Increase prednisolone to the last dose at which she was well-controlled (not back to 60 mg), typically 20–25 mg/day, and review urgently
Start azathioprine as a steroid-sparing agent and leave prednisolone at 15 mg
Admit to hospital for IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3 days as the relapse is severe
explanation: This patient has a GCA relapse — return of characteristic symptoms with rising inflammatory markers during steroid taper. The management of a GCA relapse without visual symptoms is to increase prednisolone to the dose at which symptoms were last controlled (the previous well-controlled dose, typically 20–25 mg/day in this setting), not to restart the full 60 mg induction dose. EULAR and BSR guidelines recommend returning to the dose that controlled symptoms previously. Urgent clinical review is needed due to the risk of visual loss. IV methylprednisolone is reserved for ocular/cranial nerve involvement.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Simply watching and waiting is unacceptable in a GCA relapse — rising symptoms with elevated inflammatory markers herald the risk of ischaemic optic neuropathy and sudden blindness if untreated.
Stopping prednisolone would expose the patient to maximal risk of visual loss — steroids must not be discontinued during active GCA; they are the cornerstone of treatment.

Returning to the last effective controlling dose (20–25 mg) is correct — this is the guideline-recommended approach for non-sight-threatening GCA relapse; it avoids the unnecessary toxicity of full re-induction at 60 mg.
Azathioprine alone as a steroid-sparing agent takes weeks to work and does not address the acute relapse; introducing it without increasing prednisolone risks vision-threatening disease progression.
IV methylprednisolone is indicated for GCA with visual symptoms or cranial nerve involvement — this patient has no ocular symptoms, making oral prednisolone increase the appropriate step-up.
clinicalPearl: GCA relapse during steroid taper requires increasing prednisolone to the last effective controlling dose — not re-induction at 60 mg and not IV methylprednisolone unless visual symptoms are present; urgent review is essential given risk of ischaemic optic neuropathy.
keyClue: Return of jaw claudication + rising CRP/ESR at prednisolone 15 mg in known GCA = relapse requiring dose increase to previously effective level (~20–25 mg).
commonExamTrap: Restarting full-dose induction prednisolone (60 mg) for every relapse — guidelines recommend returning only to the last effective controlling dose for non-sight-threatening relapse.
missedConcept: GCA steroid management: induction at 40–60 mg, structured taper over 12–18 months; relapse → return to previous effective dose (not re-induction) unless ocular symptoms present.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old man with granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA, previously Wegener's) achieved clinical remission after induction with rituximab and prednisolone 18 months ago. He is now on azathioprine maintenance and prednisolone 5 mg/day. At routine follow-up, urinalysis shows new 2+ haematuria and 1+ proteinuria. Serum creatinine has risen from 82 to 142 µmol/L. PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA) titre has risen from 1:20 to 1:320. He has no nasal or pulmonary symptoms. What is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Repeat urinalysis and ANCA in 4 weeks to confirm trend before acting
Refer to nephrology urgently for renal biopsy and probable re-induction immunosuppression for GPA renal relapse
Add trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for possible urinary tract infection causing haematuria
Increase azathioprine dose and add hydroxychloroquine as an adjunct
Reassure the patient — rising ANCA titres alone do not indicate relapse and no treatment change is needed
explanation: This patient has a GPA renal relapse — new haematuria and proteinuria with rising serum creatinine and a fourfold rise in PR3-ANCA titre in a patient with known renal GPA in remission is highly indicative of active glomerulonephritis. Urgent nephrology referral and renal biopsy is indicated to confirm active vasculitis and assess chronicity, as this guides re-induction therapy. Delay risks irreversible loss of nephrons and progression to end-stage renal disease. Re-induction with rituximab (preferred over cyclophosphamide for relapsing disease) and pulse methylprednisolone is typically required.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A 4-week delay in a patient with rising creatinine and active urinary sediment in GPA risks permanent renal scarring — this clinical picture demands urgent investigation and treatment, not watchful waiting.

Urgent nephrology referral and renal biopsy is correct — the combination of rising creatinine, active urine sediment, and significant ANCA rise in known GPA mandates same-day or next-day specialist review and biopsy to guide re-induction.

Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is used as prophylaxis against Pneumocystis in GPA patients on immunosuppression, but it is not indicated for suspected UTI without symptoms; the clinical picture is systemic vasculitis relapse, not infection.

Increasing azathioprine is an insufficient response to a GPA renal relapse with rising creatinine — azathioprine is a maintenance agent, not a re-induction drug; formal re-induction with rituximab or cyclophosphamide plus steroids is required.
A fourfold rise in PR3-ANCA titre combined with active urinary sediment and rising creatinine is not simply a serological fluctuation — this is a clinical relapse requiring immediate action.
clinicalPearl: In GPA (c-ANCA/PR3-positive), rising ANCA titre combined with new haematuria/proteinuria and worsening renal function = renal relapse until proven otherwise — urgent nephrology referral and renal biopsy are needed to guide re-induction therapy.
keyClue: GPA in remission + fourfold ANCA rise + new haematuria/proteinuria + rising creatinine = renal relapse requiring urgent specialist review and probable re-induction.
commonExamTrap: Treating rising ANCA titre as an isolated laboratory finding and not acting — ANCA rise plus new urinary abnormalities and worsening renal function is clinical relapse, not laboratory noise.
missedConcept: GPA renal relapse monitoring: urinalysis (dipstick + microscopy), serum creatinine, and ANCA titre at every visit; rising ANCA with clinical/biochemical changes mandates urgent biopsy — not watchful waiting.

topic: Giant cell arteritis — sudden visual loss
clinicalStem: A 68-year-old woman with a 3-week history of left temporal headache and scalp tenderness presents to the GP reporting sudden painless complete loss of vision in her left eye that occurred 1 hour ago. On examination, her left eye has no light perception; afferent pupillary defect is present on the left. Right vision is intact. ESR is 94 mm/hr. Her BP is 138/82 mmHg. She is on no regular medications. What is the SINGLE most important immediate action?
options: Arrange urgent ophthalmology appointment tomorrow morning for formal fundoscopy
Request temporal artery biopsy today before starting any treatment — a diagnosis must be confirmed first
Administer high-dose IV methylprednisolone (500–1000 mg) immediately and arrange same-day emergency hospital transfer
Start oral prednisolone 60 mg today and arrange urgent temporal artery biopsy within 7–10 days
Refer to neurology to exclude stroke — painless monocular vision loss should be investigated for carotid emboli first
explanation: Acute monocular vision loss in a patient with symptoms of giant cell arteritis (headache, scalp tenderness, elevated ESR) is anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy due to GCA — a true ocular emergency. Immediate high-dose IV methylprednisolone (500–1000 mg/day for 3 days) is the treatment of choice when vision is already lost in one eye, as it reduces the risk of contralateral eye involvement (estimated 25–50% risk within days if untreated). The patient requires same-day hospital transfer. Temporal artery biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks of starting steroids without affecting diagnostic yield — treatment must NEVER be withheld to wait for biopsy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: An ophthalmology appointment tomorrow morning is unacceptably delayed — the other eye is at 25–50% risk of losing vision within hours to days; this is an emergency requiring same-day IV steroids.

Waiting for a biopsy before treating is dangerous and incorrect — temporal artery biopsy remains positive for up to 2 weeks after starting steroids; treatment must begin immediately to protect the other eye.

IV methylprednisolone immediately plus emergency hospital transfer is correct — this is the standard of care for GCA with established visual loss to protect the remaining eye; oral prednisolone is insufficient at this stage.

Oral prednisolone 60 mg is the treatment for GCA without visual involvement; once vision is lost in one eye, IV methylprednisolone is required for maximal protection of the contralateral eye — oral therapy is not aggressive enough.

GCA is the correct diagnosis here — all clinical features (age, headache, scalp tenderness, raised ESR, painless monocular loss) point to GCA; neurological stroke evaluation should not delay immediate steroid therapy.
clinicalPearl: GCA with acute vision loss = ocular emergency: start IV methylprednisolone immediately and transfer to hospital — do NOT withhold treatment to wait for biopsy, as the contralateral eye is at 25–50% risk of blindness within 24–48 hours.
keyClue: Painless sudden monocular vision loss + headache + scalp tenderness + ESR 94 in a 68-year-old = GCA with anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy — treat NOW with IV methylprednisolone.
commonExamTrap: Delaying steroids to confirm biopsy — temporal artery biopsy remains positive for up to 2 weeks after starting steroids; treatment must NEVER be withheld to await a diagnosis in this emergency.
missedConcept: GCA with visual loss is an ocular emergency requiring IV methylprednisolone (not oral) to protect the contralateral eye; biopsy diagnostic yield is preserved for 2 weeks after steroid initiation.
redFlagReason: Acute monocular vision loss in GCA is an emergency — the contralateral eye is at 25–50% risk of permanent blindness within 24–48 hours without high-dose IV corticosteroid therapy; any delay in treatment risks irreversible bilateral blindness.
